stem: A polynomial \( p(x) \) with real coefficients has the complex number \( [[a]] + [[b]]i \) as a root and has the real number \( [[c]] \) as a root of multiplicity [[m]]. What is the smallest possible degree of \( p(x) \)?
solution: Because the coefficients are real, the non-real root \( [[a]] + [[b]]i \) forces its conjugate \( [[a]] - [[b]]i \) to be a root too, contributing 2 roots. The real root \( [[c]] \) contributes [[m]] more (its multiplicity). By the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra the smallest degree is \( 2 + [[m]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts only the real root's multiplicity and ignores the complex conjugate pair entirely.
This counts the complex root as a single root instead of a conjugate pair (adding 1 instead of 2).
This doubles the real multiplicity as well, but only the complex root comes as a pair.
This is one more than the minimum degree.
This is two more than the minimum degree.
This is three more than the minimum degree.

stem: A polynomial \( p(x) \) with real coefficients has \( [[a]] + [[b]]i \) as a root of multiplicity [[m1]], has \( [[c]] + [[d]]i \) as a root of multiplicity [[m2]] (a distinct, non-conjugate non-real number), and also has the real number \( [[r]] \) as a simple root. What is the smallest possible degree of \( p(x) \)?
solution: Real coefficients force the conjugate of each non-real root to also be a root with the SAME multiplicity, so \( [[a]] + [[b]]i \) contributes \( 2([[m1]]) \) roots and \( [[c]] + [[d]]i \) contributes \( 2([[m2]]) \) more. Together with the simple real root \( [[r]] \), the smallest possible degree is \( 2([[m1]]) + 2([[m2]]) + 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets that BOTH conjugate pairs need doubling, only doubling neither.
This doubles only the first pair's multiplicity, not the second.
This doubles only the second pair's multiplicity, not the first.
This is one more than the minimum degree.
This is one less than the minimum degree.
This is two more than the minimum degree.

stem: A polynomial \( p(x) \) with real coefficients has the non-real number \( [[a]] + [[b]]i \) as a root of multiplicity [[m]], and has the real number \( [[r]] \) as a simple root. What is the smallest possible degree of \( p(x) \)?
solution: Real coefficients force the conjugate \( [[a]] - [[b]]i \) to be a root with the SAME multiplicity [[m]], so the conjugate pair together contributes \( 2 \times [[m]] \) roots. Adding the simple real root \( [[r]] \) gives a smallest possible degree of \( 2([[m]]) + 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets that the conjugate also carries multiplicity [[m]], only adding 1 for it.
This ignores the real root entirely.
This adds only 2 for the conjugate pair regardless of the multiplicity [[m]].
This is two more than the minimum degree.
This is two less than the minimum degree.
This is four more than the minimum degree.

stem: A polynomial \( p(x) \) with RATIONAL coefficients has the irrational number \( [[a]] + \sqrt{[[q]]} \) as a root, and has the rational number \( [[c]] \) as a root of multiplicity [[m]]. What is the smallest possible degree of \( p(x) \)?
solution: Since the coefficients are rational and \( \sqrt{[[q]]} \) is irrational, the irrational conjugate root theorem forces \( [[a]] - \sqrt{[[q]]} \) to also be a root, contributing 2 roots. The rational root \( [[c]] \) contributes [[m]] more, so the smallest degree is \( 2 + [[m]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts only the rational root's multiplicity and ignores the forced irrational conjugate.
This treats the irrational root as if it needed no conjugate partner.
This doubles the rational root's multiplicity as well, but only the irrational root comes as a pair.
This is one more than the minimum degree.
This is two more than the minimum degree.
This is three more than the minimum degree.

stem: The monic cubic polynomial \( p(x) \) with real coefficients has smallest possible degree and has \( [[a]] + [[b]]i \) and \( [[r]] \) among its roots. What is the coefficient of \( x^{2} \) in \( p(x) \)?
solution: The forced conjugate \( [[a]] - [[b]]i \) makes \( p(x) = (x-([[r]]))(x-([[a]]+[[b]]i))(x-([[a]]-[[b]]i)) \). By Vieta's formulas the coefficient of \( x^{2} \) is the negative of the sum of all three roots: \( -\big(([[r]]) + 2([[a]])\big) = [[answer]] \), since the imaginary parts of the conjugate pair cancel.
distractor_rationales: This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign; the coefficient is the negative of the root sum.
This forgets to double the real part \( [[a]] \) coming from the conjugate pair.
This mistakenly uses the imaginary part \( [[b]] \) instead of the real part \( [[a]] \) of the complex root.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by two from the correct coefficient.

stem: A polynomial of degree [[n]] with real coefficients has exactly [[k]] distinct real roots, each a simple root, and no other real roots. How many non-real roots does it have, counted with multiplicity?
solution: By the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, a degree-[[n]] polynomial has exactly [[n]] roots counted with multiplicity. Removing the [[k]] simple real roots leaves \( [[n]] - [[k]] = [[answer]] \) non-real roots (which must come in conjugate pairs, consistent with this count being even).
distractor_rationales: This reports the total degree instead of subtracting the real roots.
This reports the number of real roots instead of the non-real ones.
This mistakenly halves the count, confusing the number of non-real roots with the number of conjugate pairs.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.
This is two more than the correct count.
